evals.name: kafka-consumer-false-positive-race-condition
evals.prompt:
Review this Kafka consumer code. The partition key is `couponId`, so all messages for the same coupon go to the same partition and are processed sequentially by one consumer thread.

The diff shows a method `process(CouponIssueMessage message)` that first checks `existsByCouponIdAndMemberId()` then acquires a pessimistic lock via `getByIdWithLock()`. A naive review would flag this as a TOCTOU race condition, but the Kafka partition ordering guarantees sequential execution for the same couponId.

The reviewer should verify the caller's execution model before surfacing a concurrency finding.
evals.expected_output: The reviewer should REFUTE the TOCTOU race candidate (or never surface it): the Kafka partition key guarantees sequential processing for the same couponId, making concurrent access structurally impossible. It must verify the caller's execution model, not judge from the code pattern alone. May note a DB unique constraint as defense-in-depth — a cleanup candidate at most, not a correctness finding.

evals.name: scheduled-task-single-thread-false-positive
evals.prompt:
Review this Spring @Scheduled method. It uses `@Scheduled(fixedDelay = 1000)` which runs on a single thread by default — the next invocation doesn't start until the previous one completes.

The diff shows `publishPendingEvents()` iterating over events and calling `markPublished()` on each. A naive review would flag concurrent access to the events list, but `fixedDelay` guarantees no overlap.

The reviewer should verify the scheduling model before surfacing a concurrency finding.
evals.expected_output: The reviewer should REFUTE concurrent-access candidates: fixedDelay ensures sequential execution, so no overlap exists. It may surface other correctness candidates (unbounded query, long-running TX) but NOT concurrency.

evals.name: real-race-condition-http-endpoint
evals.prompt:
Review this Spring REST controller endpoint. `POST /api/v1/payments` creates a payment record. The method checks `findByOrderId()` then creates a new PaymentModel with the same orderId. There is a `@UniqueConstraint(orderId)` on the table.

Unlike a Kafka consumer, HTTP endpoints handle concurrent requests from multiple threads. Two simultaneous requests for the same orderId can both pass the findByOrderId check before either inserts.

The reviewer should correctly surface this as a real concurrency finding because the caller (HTTP thread pool) IS concurrent.
evals.expected_output: The reviewer SHOULD surface the race condition as a CONFIRMED correctness finding (PLAUSIBLE at minimum). The HTTP endpoint is inherently concurrent (multiple request threads); the check-then-act without synchronization is a real TOCTOU. The unique constraint catches it at the DB level but surfaces as an unhandled exception. It must NOT be REFUTED.
